expanded_description: A clear introduction to bacterial structure for secondary school biology. This covers what bacteria are made of—their cell walls, membranes, and surface features—and explains how these structures help bacteria survive, move, and cause disease. It also explains the difference between Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria and why this matters for antibiotics.

core_concept.principle: Bacteria are single-celled organisms with a simple internal structure (no nucleus, no membrane-bound organelles) but a complex outer envelope. Their cell wall gives them shape and protects them from bursting. Some bacteria have flagella for swimming, pili for sticking to surfaces, and capsules for hiding from the immune system.
core_concept.explanation:
Bacteria are the most numerous organisms on Earth. A single gram of soil can contain millions of them. They live in every habitat—soil, water, inside plants and animals, and even in extreme environments like hot springs and deep-sea vents. Their success comes from a simple but versatile body plan.

Bacteria are prokaryotes. This means they have no nucleus. Their DNA floats freely in the cytoplasm in a region called the nucleoid. They have no mitochondria, no chloroplasts, no endoplasmic reticulum. But they are not simple—they have highly organized structures that let them do everything they need to do.

The most important structure for a bacterium is the cell wall. Most bacteria have a cell wall made of a material called peptidoglycan. This is like a mesh bag that surrounds the cell. It gives the bacterium its shape (round, rod, or spiral) and prevents it from bursting when water enters the cell. If you remove the cell wall, the bacterium will swell up and burst.

Bacteria are divided into two major groups based on their cell wall structure. Gram-positive bacteria have a thick, single-layered cell wall. They stain purple with the Gram stain. Gram-negative bacteria have a thin cell wall and an extra outer membrane. They stain pink. This difference is important because many antibiotics work by attacking the cell wall, and Gram-negative bacteria are often harder to kill because of their outer membrane.

Outside the cell wall, some bacteria have a capsule—a slimy layer that protects them from the immune system. This is why some bacteria are hard for your body to fight. Some bacteria have flagella—long, whip-like tails that spin like propellers to move the cell. Some have pili—hair-like structures that help them stick to surfaces or to other bacteria.

Inside the cell, bacteria have all the machinery they need to live. Ribosomes (smaller than ours) make proteins. Plasmids (small circles of DNA) carry extra genes, often for antibiotic resistance. Some bacteria form endospores—tough, dormant structures that can survive boiling, radiation, and disinfectants.

Understanding bacterial structure helps us understand how they cause disease, how antibiotics work, and how to control infections.

image.alt: A diagram of a bacterium showing the cell wall, plasma membrane, DNA (nucleoid), ribosomes, flagella, pili, and capsule
image.caption: Bacterial structure: Cell wall (shape and protection), Plasma membrane (controls what enters), DNA (nucleoid), Ribosomes (make proteins), Flagella (swimming), Pili (sticking), Capsule (hides from immune system).

sections.introduction:
Bacteria are everywhere. They are in the soil, in the water, in the air, and on your skin. There are more bacteria on your body than there are human cells. Most are harmless, and many are helpful. Bacteria help us digest food, make vitamins, and protect us from harmful germs. But some bacteria cause disease—strep throat, pneumonia, food poisoning, and tuberculosis.

Bacteria are tiny. You cannot see them without a microscope. A typical bacterium is about 1 to 5 micrometers long—about 1/1000 of a millimeter. You could fit hundreds on the period at the end of this sentence. But despite their small size, bacteria have complex structures that allow them to thrive in almost any environment.

Bacteria are prokaryotes. This means their DNA is not enclosed in a nucleus. It floats freely in the cytoplasm in a region called the nucleoid. They have no mitochondria, no chloroplasts, and no other membrane-bound organelles. But they are not simple. They have a cell wall that gives them shape and protects them from bursting. They have a cell membrane that controls what enters and leaves. They have ribosomes that make proteins. Some have flagella to swim, pili to stick to surfaces, and capsules to hide from the immune system.

The cell wall is one of the most important structures for bacteria. It is made of a material called peptidoglycan—a mesh of sugar chains linked by short peptides. This mesh gives the cell its shape and prevents it from bursting when water enters. If you remove the cell wall, the bacterium swells up and bursts. This is why antibiotics that attack the cell wall (like penicillin) are so effective.

Bacteria come in three basic shapes. Cocci are round (like a ball). Bacilli are rod-shaped (like a sausage). Spirilla are spiral-shaped (like a corkscrew). The shape is determined by the cell wall.

Bacteria can be divided into two major groups based on how they react to the Gram stain. Gram-positive bacteria have a thick cell wall that traps the purple dye. They look purple under the microscope. Gram-negative bacteria have a thin cell wall and an extra outer membrane that blocks the dye. They look pink. This difference is important because it affects how bacteria respond to antibiotics.

Understanding bacterial structure helps us understand how they live, how they cause disease, and how to kill them.

introduction_image.alt: Images of bacterial shapes: cocci (round), bacilli (rod), spirilla (spiral)
introduction_image.caption: Bacterial shapes: Cocci (round), Bacilli (rod-shaped), Spirilla (spiral). Shape is determined by the cell wall.

key_concept.definition: Bacteria are prokaryotic cells with a cell wall made of peptidoglycan, a plasma membrane, and DNA in a nucleoid. They have ribosomes for making proteins and may have flagella, pili, and capsules. Gram-positive bacteria have a thick cell wall; Gram-negative have a thin wall plus an outer membrane.
key_concept.context: The cell wall is essential for survival—it prevents the cell from bursting. Antibiotics like penicillin target cell wall synthesis. The Gram stain separates bacteria into two groups: Gram-positive (purple, thick wall) and Gram-negative (pink, thin wall + outer membrane). This difference affects antibiotic susceptibility and pathogenicity.

image.alt: A labeled diagram of a bacterial cell showing cell wall, plasma membrane, DNA, ribosomes, flagella, pili
image.caption: Bacterial cell: Cell wall (peptidoglycan), Plasma membrane, Nucleoid (DNA), Ribosomes, Flagella (movement), Pili (attachment), Capsule (protection).

sections.key_concept: Bacteria have a cell wall made of peptidoglycan that gives them shape and prevents bursting. Gram-positive bacteria have a thick wall; Gram-negative have a thin wall plus an outer membrane. Surface structures like flagella, pili, and capsules help bacteria move, attach, and evade the immune system.

classifications.type: The Cell Wall: Peptidoglycan and the Gram Stain
classifications.value: Understanding the cell wall explains why bacteria have different shapes, why some are harder to kill, and how antibiotics like penicillin work.
characteristics: Peptidoglycan: A mesh of sugar chains (NAG and NAM) cross-linked by short peptides. Provides strength and shape.
Gram-positive: Thick peptidoglycan layer (20-80 nm). No outer membrane. Stains purple. Examples: Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, Bacillus.
Gram-negative: Thin peptidoglycan layer (2-7 nm). Outer membrane with lipopolysaccharide (LPS). Stains pink. Examples: Escherichia coli, Salmonella, Pseudomonas.
Outer membrane: An extra lipid layer outside the thin peptidoglycan. Blocks many antibiotics and detergents. Contains LPS (endotoxin) that triggers fever and shock.
Periplasm: Space between inner and outer membranes (in Gram-negatives). Contains enzymes that break down nutrients and destroy some antibiotics.
classifications.behavior:
The cell wall is what gives bacteria their shape. If you remove the cell wall, the bacterium becomes round and eventually bursts. This is because water constantly enters the cell by osmosis. The cell wall pushes back, preventing the cell from swelling too much. It is like a bicycle tire—the air inside pushes out, but the tire keeps it from exploding.

Peptidoglycan is made of long sugar chains (NAG and NAM) linked together by peptide bridges. The sugar chains run around the cell like hoops on a barrel. The peptide bridges connect the hoops, creating a strong mesh. This is why the cell wall can resist pressure from inside the cell.

The Gram stain is named after Hans Christian Gram, who developed it in 1884. Gram-positive bacteria have a thick, single-layered cell wall that traps the purple dye. Gram-negative bacteria have a thin cell wall and an outer membrane that blocks the dye. After washing, the Gram-positive cells stay purple; the Gram-negative cells lose the purple and take up the pink counterstain.

The Gram stain is important because it tells us about the bacterium's structure. Gram-negative bacteria have an outer membrane that protects them from many antibiotics and detergents. This is why some infections are harder to treat. The outer membrane also contains lipopolysaccharide (LPS), which is a toxin that can cause fever and shock when released into the bloodstream.

examples.explanation:
Penicillin was the first antibiotic discovered. It works by stopping bacteria from making their cell walls. The target is an enzyme called transpeptidase (penicillin-binding protein) that links the peptide bridges in peptidoglycan. Penicillin looks like the end of the peptide chain (D-Ala-D-Ala). It fits into the active site of the enzyme and blocks it. The bacteria cannot cross-link their peptidoglycan. Their cell walls are weak. When water enters, they swell and burst.

This is why penicillin is effective against Gram-positive bacteria—they have thick cell walls and rely heavily on peptidoglycan. Gram-negative bacteria have an outer membrane that blocks penicillin, which is why they are less sensitive. Some bacteria make an enzyme called β-lactamase that breaks the penicillin ring. This is how bacteria become resistant. We now use penicillin combined with β-lactamase inhibitors (like clavulanic acid) to overcome resistance.

image.alt: Penicillin blocking transpeptidase, preventing cell wall cross-linking, leading to bacterial lysis
image.caption: Penicillin: Blocks transpeptidase enzyme → no cross-linking in peptidoglycan → weak cell wall → water enters → bacterium bursts. Gram-positive bacteria are more sensitive because they have thick walls.

examples.explanation:
The Gram stain uses four chemicals. First, crystal violet (purple dye) stains all bacteria purple. Then, iodine is added. It forms a complex with the crystal violet, making it stick. Next, alcohol is used to wash. Gram-positive bacteria have a thick peptidoglycan layer that traps the crystal violet-iodine complex. They stay purple. Gram-negative bacteria have a thin peptidoglycan layer and an outer membrane. The alcohol dissolves the outer membrane and washes away the purple dye. They become colorless. Finally, safranin (pink dye) is added. Gram-negative bacteria pick up the pink color. Gram-positive bacteria stay purple.

This simple test tells us a lot about the bacterium. It tells us about the structure of the cell wall. It helps doctors choose the right antibiotic. For example, a Gram-positive infection might be treated with penicillin; a Gram-negative infection might need a different antibiotic that can get through the outer membrane.

image.alt: Steps of Gram stain: crystal violet, iodine, alcohol wash, safranin; Gram-positive purple, Gram-negative pink
image.caption: Gram stain: 1. Crystal violet (all purple). 2. Iodine (forms complex). 3. Alcohol wash (Gram-positive hold dye, Gram-negative lose dye). 4. Safranin (Gram-negative turn pink, Gram-positive stay purple).

classifications.type: Flagella, Pili, and Capsules: Surface Structures for Survival
classifications.value: Understanding surface structures explains how bacteria move, stick to surfaces, and evade the immune system.
characteristics: Flagella: Long, whip-like tails that rotate like propellers. Made of a protein called flagellin. Powered by a motor in the cell membrane. Used for swimming (chemotaxis).
Pili (fimbriae): Short, hair-like structures made of protein. Used for sticking to surfaces (adhesion) and to each other. Some pili are used for conjugation (transferring DNA).
Capsule: A slimy layer outside the cell wall. Made of polysaccharides (sugars) or polypeptides. Protects against drying, antibiotics, and the immune system.
S-layer: A crystalline protein layer on the surface of some bacteria. Provides protection and helps with attachment.
Biofilm: A community of bacteria stuck to a surface and wrapped in a slimy matrix (mostly polysaccharides). Bacteria in biofilms are very hard to kill.
classifications.behavior:
Flagella are like tiny propellers. The base of the flagellum is a motor embedded in the cell membrane. It spins using energy from the flow of protons (proton motive force). The flagellum can spin clockwise or counterclockwise. When it spins counterclockwise, the flagella bundle together and the bacterium swims in a straight line (run). When it spins clockwise, the bundle falls apart and the bacterium tumbles, changing direction. By controlling the frequency of tumbles, the bacterium can swim toward food and away from toxins. This is called chemotaxis.

Pili are for sticking. Some pili are used to attach to surfaces—like a bacterium attaching to a tooth (forming plaque) or to the lining of the bladder (causing infection). Other pili are used for conjugation—transferring DNA from one bacterium to another. This is how antibiotic resistance genes spread.

The capsule is a slippery coat. It protects the bacterium from the immune system. When a white blood cell tries to engulf a bacterium, the capsule makes it hard to grab. Capsules also protect against drying out and against some antibiotics. Many disease-causing bacteria have capsules. Vaccines against these bacteria (like pneumococcus) often target the capsule.

examples.process: How E. coli Swims Toward Food
examples.explanation:
E. coli has about 5-10 flagella. It lives in the gut, where nutrients are not evenly spread. It needs to swim toward higher concentrations of food (like sugars and amino acids). How does it do this?

The bacterium has sensors on its surface that detect chemicals. When it detects food, it sends a signal to the flagellar motor. The motor spins counterclockwise more often, so the bacterium runs longer and tumbles less. This biases its movement toward the food. When it moves away from food, it tumbles more, changing direction.

This is not conscious—it is a simple feedback loop. But it works. E. coli can swim up a gradient of sugar very efficiently. It is one of the simplest forms of navigation in nature.

This ability helps E. coli find nutrients in the gut. It also helps pathogenic E. coli find sites to infect.

image.alt: E. coli swimming toward food: counterclockwise spin (run) and clockwise spin (tumble)
image.caption: Chemotaxis: Counterclockwise spin (flagella bundle, run) → moves toward food. Clockwise spin (flagella separate, tumble) → changes direction. The bacterium biases runs toward food.

examples.explanation:
Streptococcus pneumoniae is a bacterium that causes pneumonia, meningitis, and ear infections. It has a thick capsule made of polysaccharides. This capsule is the key to its ability to cause disease.

When the bacterium enters the body, white blood cells try to engulf it. The capsule makes the surface slippery—the white blood cells cannot grab it. The capsule also hides the bacterial proteins that the immune system would recognize. The bacterium can multiply and spread before the immune system catches up.

There are over 90 different types of S. pneumoniae, each with a different capsule. The immune system makes antibodies against one capsule type, but not against others. This is why you can get pneumonia more than once. It is also why the pneumonia vaccine contains capsule material from many different types.

The capsule is a target for vaccines. The pneumococcal vaccine contains pieces of the capsule (polysaccharides) from the most common types. The immune system learns to recognize these capsules. If you are later infected, your antibodies will coat the capsule, making the bacterium easy for white blood cells to grab and destroy.

image.alt: Streptococcus pneumoniae with capsule resisting phagocytosis; antibodies opsonizing the capsule
image.caption: Capsule: Polysaccharide coat makes bacterium slippery; white blood cells cannot grab it. Antibodies against the capsule (opsonization) help white blood cells engulf it. Vaccines target the capsule.

classifications.type: Internal Structures: DNA, Ribosomes, and Plasmids
classifications.value: Understanding internal structures explains how bacteria store information, make proteins, and share genes.
characteristics: Nucleoid: Region where the bacterial chromosome (a single circular DNA molecule) is located. No nuclear membrane.
Plasmids: Small, circular DNA molecules separate from the chromosome. Carry extra genes, often for antibiotic resistance or toxins.
Ribosomes: 70S ribosomes (smaller than eukaryotic 80S ribosomes). Make proteins. Target for many antibiotics.
Inclusions: Storage granules for nutrients (starch, phosphate, sulfur). Help bacteria survive when food is scarce.
Endospores: Tough, dormant structures formed by some bacteria (Bacillus, Clostridium). Can survive boiling, radiation, and disinfectants.
classifications.behavior:
The bacterial chromosome is a single, circular DNA molecule. It is about 4-5 million base pairs long—enough to code for about 4,000-5,000 proteins. The DNA is not free-floating; it is organized by proteins that keep it folded and compact. This region is called the nucleoid.

Plasmids are small DNA circles that carry extra genes. A bacterium can have several plasmids. They often carry genes that are helpful in certain environments—antibiotic resistance, toxin production, or the ability to digest unusual food. Plasmids can be transferred between bacteria through a process called conjugation. This is how antibiotic resistance spreads so quickly.

Ribosomes are the protein factories. Bacterial ribosomes are 70S (S stands for Svedberg unit, a measure of size). Human ribosomes are 80S. This difference is important because many antibiotics target bacterial ribosomes without affecting human ribosomes. Streptomycin, tetracycline, and erythromycin all work by blocking bacterial protein synthesis.

Endospores are one of the most resistant structures in nature. Bacteria like Bacillus and Clostridium can form endospores when conditions are bad. The bacterium copies its DNA, surrounds it with a thick coat, and then the rest of the cell dies, leaving the spore. The spore can survive boiling, radiation, and disinfectants. It can stay dormant for centuries. When conditions improve, it germinates and grows into a normal bacterium. This is why it is so hard to kill anthrax or tetanus spores.

examples.explanation:
Antibiotic resistance often spreads through plasmids. Imagine a bacterium that has a plasmid carrying a gene for resistance to penicillin. When it divides, the plasmid is copied and passed to its daughter cells. But plasmids can also move between bacteria.

Some plasmids carry genes for making a sex pilus—a tube that connects two bacteria. The plasmid copies itself and sends a copy through the pilus to the other bacterium. This is called conjugation. The recipient bacterium now has the resistance plasmid and can pass it to others.

This is why antibiotic resistance can spread quickly. A single resistant bacterium can transfer its resistance genes to many other bacteria. This happens not only within the same species but sometimes between different species.

Because of conjugation, resistance to an antibiotic can appear in bacteria that have never been exposed to it. This is why we need to use antibiotics carefully—overuse creates selective pressure that favors resistant bacteria, and those resistance genes can spread.

image.alt: Plasmid transfer by conjugation: sex pilus connecting two bacteria, plasmid DNA moving through it
image.caption: Plasmid transfer: A bacterium with a resistance plasmid makes a sex pilus. The plasmid copies itself and transfers a copy to a recipient. Resistance spreads quickly through bacterial populations.

examples.explanation:
Bacillus anthracis, the bacterium that causes anthrax, can form endospores. These spores are incredibly tough. They can survive in soil for decades. They resist boiling, freezing, and many disinfectants. This is why anthrax spores can be used as biological weapons—they are easy to spread and hard to kill.

When conditions are bad (lack of food, drying out), the bacterium starts to form a spore. It copies its DNA and surrounds it with layers of protective coat. The core of the spore is dehydrated and contains high levels of calcium and dipicolinic acid, which stabilize the DNA. The spore can survive for years.

When conditions improve (nutrients, moisture), the spore germinates. It takes up water, breaks down the coat, and starts growing. Within hours, it becomes a normal, multiplying bacterium.

Clostridium tetani (tetanus) and Clostridium botulinum (botulism) also form spores. These are found in soil. If a wound is contaminated with soil, the spores can germinate and cause disease. This is why we get tetanus shots after stepping on a rusty nail—the spores may be in the soil on the nail.

image.alt: Endospore formation stages: vegetative cell, spore coat formation, mature spore, germination
image.caption: Endospores: When conditions are bad, bacterium copies DNA, builds thick coat, and forms dormant spore. Spores survive boiling, radiation, disinfectants. When conditions improve, spore germinates and grows.

principles.principle: The Cell Wall Prevents Bursting and Determines Shape
principles.statement: The cell wall is essential for bacterial survival. It resists the internal pressure from water entering the cell. Without it, the cell would swell and burst.
principles.application:
Bacteria live in watery environments. Water constantly moves into the cell by osmosis because the inside of the cell has more dissolved particles (salts, sugars) than the outside. This creates pressure—about 20 atmospheres in E. coli. That is like the pressure inside a car tire. Without a strong cell wall, the cell would explode.

The cell wall is made of peptidoglycan—a mesh of sugar chains cross-linked by peptides. This mesh is like chain mail—strong but flexible. It allows the cell to grow and change shape while still resisting pressure.

Penicillin works by blocking the formation of the cross-links. The bacterium tries to grow, but its new cell wall is weak. Water rushes in, and the cell bursts. This is why penicillin is so effective against bacteria that are actively growing.

Gram-positive bacteria have thick cell walls, so they are more sensitive to penicillin. Gram-negative bacteria have an outer membrane that blocks penicillin, so they are less sensitive. This is why we need different antibiotics for different types of bacteria.
principles.example: If you put a bacterium in distilled water, it will swell. But it will not burst—the cell wall holds it together. If you treat it with lysozyme (an enzyme in tears and saliva that breaks peptidoglycan), the cell wall is destroyed, and the bacterium bursts. This is why tears help prevent eye infections—they contain lysozyme that breaks down bacterial cell walls.
principles.analogy: The cell wall is like the tire on a bicycle. The air inside the tire pushes outward, but the rubber keeps it from exploding. If you cut the tire, it bursts. Penicillin cuts the tire (prevents cross-linking), so the cell bursts.

image.alt: Water entering a bacterium, cell wall resisting pressure; without cell wall, bacterium bursts
image.caption: Cell wall: Water enters by osmosis (pressure inside). Cell wall resists pressure. Without cell wall, bacterium swells and bursts. Lysozyme (in tears) destroys cell walls.

principles.principle: Gram-Positive and Gram-Negative Bacteria Differ in Structure and Drug Sensitivity
principles.statement: The Gram stain separates bacteria into two groups with different cell envelope structures. Gram-positive bacteria have a thick cell wall; Gram-negative bacteria have a thin cell wall plus an outer membrane. This affects how they respond to antibiotics and how they cause disease.
principles.application:
Gram-positive bacteria have a thick layer of peptidoglycan that traps the purple dye. They are sensitive to penicillin and other antibiotics that target the cell wall. Their cell wall also contains teichoic acids, which help them stick to surfaces.

Gram-negative bacteria have a thin layer of peptidoglycan and an outer membrane made of lipopolysaccharide (LPS). The outer membrane blocks many antibiotics, detergents, and disinfectants. This is why Gram-negative infections are often harder to treat. The LPS can also cause fever and shock when released into the bloodstream—this is called endotoxin.

The Gram stain is a quick way to tell which group a bacterium belongs to. This helps doctors choose antibiotics before the exact species is known. For a Gram-positive infection, penicillin or vancomycin might be used. For a Gram-negative infection, a drug that can penetrate the outer membrane (like a cephalosporin or aminoglycoside) is needed.
principles.example:
E. coli is Gram-negative. It has an outer membrane that blocks penicillin. This is why penicillin does not work against E. coli infections. To treat E. coli, we use antibiotics that can get through the outer membrane—like ciprofloxacin or ceftriaxone. Some antibiotics (like tetracycline) can pass through porins (protein channels) in the outer membrane. Others (like polymyxin) disrupt the outer membrane itself.

Staphylococcus aureus is Gram-positive. It has no outer membrane, so penicillin can reach its cell wall. But many strains of S. aureus make β-lactamase, an enzyme that destroys penicillin. This is why we use methicillin (a penicillin that resists β-lactamase) or vancomycin (which works differently) for these resistant strains.
principles.analogy: Gram-positive bacteria are like knights in plate armor (thick cell wall). Gram-negative bacteria are like knights with chain mail (thin wall) and a shield (outer membrane). The shield blocks many weapons (antibiotics).

image.alt: Comparison of Gram-positive (thick cell wall) and Gram-negative (thin wall + outer membrane) structures
image.caption: Gram-positive: Thick peptidoglycan, no outer membrane. Sensitive to penicillin. Gram-negative: Thin peptidoglycan, outer membrane with LPS. Resistant to many antibiotics. Outer membrane blocks drugs.

principles.principle: Surface Structures Enable Movement, Attachment, and Immune Evasion
principles.statement: Bacteria use flagella to swim, pili to stick to surfaces, and capsules to hide from the immune system. These structures are often essential for causing disease.
principles.application:
Flagella let bacteria move toward food and away from toxins. This is important for bacteria that live in changing environments. Pathogenic bacteria use flagella to swim to sites of infection.

Pili let bacteria attach to surfaces. This is essential for bacteria that colonize the body. Without pili, bacteria would be washed away by urine, mucus, or blood flow. Pili also allow bacteria to form biofilms—communities of bacteria stuck to surfaces and wrapped in a slimy matrix. Biofilms are very hard to kill with antibiotics.

Capsules hide bacteria from the immune system. White blood cells cannot grab bacteria with capsules. The capsule also prevents antibodies from binding. Many vaccines work by targeting the capsule—they teach the immune system to recognize it. Then, when the bacterium tries to infect, antibodies coat the capsule, and white blood cells can grab it.
principles.example:
Neisseria meningitidis (meningococcus) causes meningitis. It has a capsule that protects it from the immune system. There are several types of meningococcus, each with a different capsule. Vaccines against meningococcus are made from the capsule polysaccharides. The vaccine teaches the immune system to recognize the capsule. If you are later infected, your antibodies will coat the capsule, and your white blood cells can destroy the bacteria.

In contrast, bacteria without capsules (like some E. coli strains) are more easily killed by the immune system. They often cause less severe disease or only cause disease in people with weak immune systems.
principles.analogy: Flagella are propellers. Pili are Velcro. Capsules are camouflage. Bacteria use these tools to move, stick, and hide.

image.alt: Bacterium with flagella (swimming), pili (sticking to surface), capsule (hiding from immune cell)
image.caption: Surface structures: Flagella (swim toward food), Pili (stick to surfaces), Capsule (hide from immune system). Each helps bacteria survive and cause disease.

components.definition: Bacteria have several key components: cell wall (protection), plasma membrane (control), flagella (movement), pili (attachment), capsule (immune evasion), DNA (genetic information), ribosomes (protein synthesis), and plasmids (extra genes).
components.equation: $\text{Bacterial survival} = \text{Cell wall strength} + \text{Flagella movement} + \text{Pili attachment} + \text{Capsule protection}$
components.interpretation: Bacteria survive because they have a strong cell wall that prevents bursting, flagella to find food, pili to stick to good surfaces, and capsules to hide from predators. Each part is important. If any part is missing, the bacterium is less fit.

image.alt: A diagram of a bacterium with all key components labeled
image.caption: Key components: Cell wall (strength), Plasma membrane (control), Flagella (movement), Pili (attachment), Capsule (protection), DNA (instructions), Ribosomes (protein factories).

categories.description: Many antibiotics work by attacking bacterial structures that are different from human structures. This allows them to kill bacteria without harming us.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Cell wall antibiotics: Penicillins, cephalosporins, and vancomycin block peptidoglycan synthesis. They are effective against Gram-positive bacteria (which have thick walls) but less effective against Gram-negative bacteria (which have an outer membrane that blocks them).

Protein synthesis antibiotics: Tetracyclines, macrolides (erythromycin), and aminoglycosides (streptomycin) bind to bacterial ribosomes (70S) and block protein production. Human ribosomes are 80S, so these drugs are selective.

DNA synthesis antibiotics: Quinolones (ciprofloxacin) block DNA gyrase, an enzyme bacteria use to supercoil their DNA. Humans have a different enzyme, so quinolones are selective.

Cell membrane antibiotics: Polymyxins (colistin) disrupt the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria. They are used as a last resort for resistant infections because they can also damage human cells.

examples.explanation:
Vancomycin is a glycopeptide antibiotic used for serious Gram-positive infections, especially MRSA (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus). It works differently from penicillin. Instead of blocking the enzyme that makes the cross-links, it binds directly to the end of the peptide chain (D-Ala-D-Ala). The enzyme cannot reach the chain, so cross-linking does not happen. The cell wall is weak, and the bacterium bursts.

Some bacteria have become resistant to vancomycin. They change the end of the peptide chain from D-Ala-D-Ala to D-Ala-D-Lac. Vancomycin does not bind to this new end, so it cannot block cross-linking. These vancomycin-resistant enterococci (VRE) are a serious problem in hospitals.

Newer antibiotics (like telavancin) have been developed to overcome this resistance. They bind more strongly or have additional mechanisms of action.

image.alt: Vancomycin binding to D-Ala-D-Ala, blocking cross-linking; resistant strain with D-Ala-D-Lac
image.caption: Vancomycin: Binds to D-Ala-D-Ala at end of peptide chain → blocks cross-linking → cell wall weak → bacterium bursts. Resistance: Bacteria change to D-Ala-D-Lac, vancomycin cannot bind.

categories.description: Biofilms are communities of bacteria stuck to a surface and wrapped in a slimy matrix. Bacteria in biofilms are much harder to kill than free-floating bacteria.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Biofilm formation starts when bacteria attach to a surface using pili or flagella. They then start producing extracellular polymeric substances (EPS)—a slimy mixture of polysaccharides, proteins, and DNA. The EPS forms a protective matrix that holds the bacteria together and shields them from antibiotics and immune cells.

Inside the biofilm, bacteria can communicate with each other using chemical signals (quorum sensing). They can share nutrients and even divide labor—some cells may be more active, others more dormant. The dormant cells (persisters) are especially hard to kill because they are not actively growing, so antibiotics that target growing cells do not affect them.

Biofilms cause many chronic infections. They form on medical devices (catheters, heart valves, joint replacements), in the lungs of people with cystic fibrosis, and on teeth (plaque). They are hard to treat because antibiotics cannot penetrate the EPS, and the bacteria inside are often dormant.

examples.explanation:
Dental plaque is a biofilm. It starts when bacteria in the mouth (especially Streptococcus mutans) stick to the tooth surface using sticky proteins. They then produce a slimy matrix of polysaccharides. Other bacteria join in, forming a complex community.

The bacteria in plaque produce acids from sugar in food. These acids dissolve tooth enamel, causing cavities. Brushing and flossing physically disrupt the biofilm. This is why brushing is important—it breaks up the matrix and removes the bacteria. Mouthwash can also help, but it cannot penetrate thick plaque.

If plaque is not removed, it can harden into tartar (calculus), which is even harder to remove. Tartar can only be removed by a dentist. This is why regular dental cleanings are important for preventing gum disease.

image.alt: Dental plaque biofilm on tooth, bacteria in slimy matrix, toothbrush disrupting biofilm
image.caption: Dental plaque: Bacteria in slimy matrix on tooth surface. Produces acid from sugar → cavities. Brushing disrupts biofilm. If not removed, plaque hardens into tartar.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Identifying Bacteria by Structure

worked_examples.title: Classifying an Unknown Bacterium
worked_examples.problem: You isolate a bacterium from a patient. Tests show: (a) It stains pink (Gram-negative). (b) It has flagella that spin. (c) It has pili that help it attach to bladder cells. (d) It has a capsule. (e) It is resistant to penicillin. (1) What does the Gram stain tell you? (2) What do the flagella do? (3) What do the pili do? (4) Why is it resistant to penicillin? (5) What kind of infection might this bacterium cause?

step_by_step.calculation: Pink stain → Gram-negative. This means it has a thin peptidoglycan layer and an outer membrane. The outer membrane blocks many antibiotics, including penicillin. Gram-negative bacteria are often harder to treat.
Flagella are for movement. They spin like propellers. The bacterium uses them to swim toward nutrients and away from toxins. This helps it find places to grow.

Pili help the bacterium attach to surfaces. In this case, they help it attach to bladder cells. This is important for causing a urinary tract infection—without pili, the bacterium would be washed away in urine.

Gram-negative bacteria are naturally resistant to penicillin because the outer membrane blocks the drug. Some also make β-lactamase, an enzyme that destroys penicillin. The outer membrane is the main barrier.

A Gram-negative bacterium that attaches to bladder cells (pili) and has a capsule (immune evasion) is likely to cause a urinary tract infection. Common examples are E. coli and Klebsiella. The capsule helps it avoid being killed by the immune system.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Gram-negative: thin cell wall + outer membrane. (2) Flagella: movement, swimming toward nutrients. (3) Pili: attach to bladder cells, prevents being washed away. (4) Penicillin resistance: outer membrane blocks drug; may also have β-lactamase. (5) Likely cause: urinary tract infection (UTI). This shows how bacterial structure helps us understand how it causes disease and why certain antibiotics work or don't work.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Bacterial structure predicts behavior: Gram-negative means outer membrane blocks many drugs; flagella means it can move; pili mean it can attach; capsule means it can evade immune system. Together, these features suggest a bacterium that causes infection in the urinary tract.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: How Bacteria Build Their Cell Wall
sections.introduction: Bacteria build their cell wall in several steps. Understanding this process reveals how antibiotics work.

process_flow.description: The building blocks of peptidoglycan are made inside the cell.
process_flow.mechanism: The cell makes NAG (N-acetylglucosamine) and NAM (N-acetylmuramic acid) from glucose. It attaches a chain of amino acids (peptides) to the NAM. These are the building blocks for the cell wall.
process_flow.result: NAG and NAM with attached peptides ready to be added to the wall.
applications: Antibiotics like fosfomycin block this step.

image.alt: NAG and NAM with peptide chain being made in cytoplasm
image.caption: Step 1: NAG and NAM are made in cytoplasm. A peptide chain is attached to NAM. These are the building blocks for the cell wall.

process_flow.description: The NAG-NAM-peptide building blocks are attached to a lipid carrier and flipped to the outside of the cell.
process_flow.mechanism: A lipid molecule (bactoprenol) picks up the NAG-NAM-peptide unit in the cytoplasm and carries it across the cell membrane. On the outside, it is released. This step requires energy.
process_flow.result: Building blocks delivered to the cell wall assembly site.
applications: Bacitracin blocks the recycling of the lipid carrier.

image.alt: Lipid carrier transporting NAG-NAM-peptide across membrane
image.caption: Step 2: Lipid carrier (bactoprenol) picks up building block in cytoplasm, flips to outside. Building block is added to growing wall. Bacitracin blocks recycling of lipid carrier.

process_flow.description: The building blocks are linked into chains, and the chains are cross-linked to make a strong mesh.
process_flow.mechanism: Enzymes called transglycosylases link the NAG-NAM units into long chains. Then transpeptidases (penicillin-binding proteins) link the peptide chains together. This cross-linking makes the wall strong.

image.alt: NAG-NAM chains being cross-linked by transpeptidase
image.caption: Step 3: Transglycosylases link NAG-NAM into chains. Transpeptidases (PBPs) cross-link peptide chains. Penicillin blocks transpeptidases → weak wall → cell bursts.

sections.definition: Bacteria have evolved amazing adaptations to survive in extreme environments. Some form endospores to survive boiling. Others live in biofilms that resist antibiotics. These adaptations make some infections very hard to treat.

process_types.energy_profile: Forming a spore takes energy, but the spore can survive for centuries without energy.
process_types.direction: Vegetative cell → stress → spore formation → dormant spore → favorable conditions → germination → vegetative cell
process_types.function: Survive extreme heat, radiation, drying, and disinfectants. Allows bacteria to wait for good conditions.
process_types.energy_source: Spore uses no energy while dormant; germination uses energy to restart metabolism.

primary_example.explanation:
Bacillus anthracis causes anthrax. It forms spores that can survive in soil for decades. These spores are very tough. They resist boiling, freezing, and many disinfectants. This is why anthrax spores are a bioterrorism concern—they can be spread as a powder and are hard to kill.

The spore has a thick coat made of protein. Inside, the DNA is protected by small acid-soluble proteins (SASPs) that bind to it and prevent damage. The core is dehydrated—most water is removed. This helps the spore survive heat. The spore also contains high levels of calcium and dipicolinic acid, which stabilize the DNA.

When conditions improve, the spore germinates. It takes up water, breaks down the coat, and starts growing. Within hours, it becomes a normal, multiplying bacterium. This is why anthrax spores can cause disease years after they were deposited.

image.alt: Bacillus anthracis spore structure: coat, core with DNA protected by SASPs and Ca-DPA
image.caption: Anthrax spore: Thick protein coat, dehydrated core, DNA protected by SASPs, calcium dipicolinate stabilizes DNA. Survives decades in soil, resists boiling. Germinates when conditions improve.

image.alt: Mechanisms of spore resistance: dehydration, SASPs, calcium dipicolinate, thick coat
image.caption: Spore resistance: Dehydration (heat resistance), SASPs (DNA protection), calcium dipicolinate (DNA stabilization), thick coat (chemical resistance). Spores can survive boiling, radiation, disinfectants.

process_types.energy_profile: Building a biofilm takes energy, but the biofilm protects the bacteria from antibiotics and immune cells.
process_types.direction: Planktonic cells → attach to surface → produce EPS → form microcolonies → mature biofilm → disperse cells
process_types.function: Protect bacteria from antibiotics, disinfectants, and immune cells. Allow bacteria to share nutrients and resist stress.
process_types.energy_source: Bacteria in biofilm use nutrients from environment; EPS production requires energy.

primary_example.process: Pseudomonas aeruginosa in Cystic Fibrosis Lungs
primary_example.explanation:
People with cystic fibrosis (CF) have thick mucus in their lungs. This mucus provides a perfect surface for bacteria to grow. Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a common pathogen in CF. It forms biofilms in the lungs that are very hard to treat.

The biofilm protects the bacteria from antibiotics. The slimy matrix (made of alginate) blocks the drugs. The bacteria deep in the biofilm are not growing, so antibiotics that target growing cells do not kill them. The bacteria can also transfer resistance genes to each other.

People with CF often need to take antibiotics for months at a time. Even then, the infection is rarely cleared. The biofilm protects the bacteria, and they can regrow when treatment stops. Researchers are looking for ways to break up biofilms—for example, using enzymes that digest the alginate matrix.

image.alt: Pseudomonas biofilm in lung, EPS matrix, bacteria in microcolonies, antibiotic molecules blocked
image.caption: Pseudomonas biofilm in CF lungs: EPS matrix blocks antibiotics; dormant cells survive treatment. Biofilm infections are very hard to cure. New treatments aim to break up the matrix.

image.alt: Biofilm formation stages: attachment, microcolony, EPS production, mature biofilm, dispersal
image.caption: Biofilm stages: Planktonic cells attach → form microcolonies → produce EPS matrix → mature biofilm with water channels → cells disperse to colonize new sites.

sections.relationship: Endospores and biofilms are two ways bacteria survive harsh conditions. Endospores are for long-term survival (years to centuries). Biofilms are for short-term survival in the presence of antibiotics and immune cells. Both are major challenges in treating bacterial infections.

sections.purpose: These are the essential vocabulary terms for understanding bacterial structure. Knowing these terms will help you describe how bacteria are built and how they survive.

activation.definition: The terms below cover the most important concepts in bacterial structure. Each term is defined simply, with context to help you remember its significance.
activation.effect: Mastering these terms will allow you to discuss bacterial structure, Gram staining, and how antibiotics work with confidence.

image.alt: A labeled diagram of a bacterium with key terms: cell wall, plasma membrane, flagella, pili, capsule, DNA, ribosomes
image.caption: Key bacterial terms: Knowing these structures helps you understand how bacteria live, move, and cause disease.

key_properties.explanation: The material that makes up the bacterial cell wall. Made of sugar chains (NAG and NAM) cross-linked by short peptides. Gives the cell strength and shape. Target of penicillin.
Bacteria with a thick peptidoglycan cell wall. Stain purple with Gram stain. No outer membrane. Generally sensitive to penicillin. Examples: Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, Bacillus.
Bacteria with a thin peptidoglycan layer and an outer membrane. Stain pink with Gram stain. Outer membrane blocks many antibiotics. Examples: E. coli, Salmonella, Pseudomonas.
An extra lipid layer outside the thin peptidoglycan in Gram-negative bacteria. Contains lipopolysaccharide (LPS). Blocks many antibiotics and detergents. LPS triggers fever and shock.
A long, whip-like tail that spins like a propeller. Used for swimming (chemotaxis). Made of flagellin protein. Powered by a motor in the cell membrane.
A short, hair-like structure on the bacterial surface. Used for attaching to surfaces (adhesion) and to other bacteria (conjugation). Important for forming biofilms and causing infection.
A slimy layer outside the cell wall. Made of polysaccharides. Protects against drying, antibiotics, and the immune system. Many vaccines target the capsule.
The region in the bacterial cell where the DNA is located. No nuclear membrane. The DNA is a single circular chromosome.
A small, circular DNA molecule separate from the main chromosome. Carries extra genes, often for antibiotic resistance or toxins. Can be transferred between bacteria by conjugation.
The protein factory of the cell. Bacterial ribosomes are 70S (smaller than human 80S ribosomes). Target of many antibiotics (tetracycline, erythromycin, streptomycin).
A tough, dormant structure formed by some bacteria (Bacillus, Clostridium). Can survive boiling, radiation, and disinfectants. Germinates when conditions improve.
A community of bacteria stuck to a surface and wrapped in a slimy matrix. Protects bacteria from antibiotics and immune cells. Causes chronic infections and dental plaque.
An enzyme that cross-links the peptides in peptidoglycan. Target of penicillin and other β-lactam antibiotics. When blocked, the cell wall is weak and the bacterium bursts.

An enzyme produced by some bacteria that breaks the β-lactam ring of penicillin and related antibiotics. Confers resistance. We combine penicillin with β-lactamase inhibitors (clavulanic acid) to overcome this.
A molecule in the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria. Consists of Lipid A (endotoxin), core sugar, and O-antigen. Triggers fever and shock when released into the bloodstream.
Communication between bacteria using chemical signals. Bacteria use it to coordinate behavior, such as biofilm formation and toxin production.

summary:
Bacteria are prokaryotic cells with a simple internal structure but a complex outer envelope. Their most important feature is the cell wall, made of peptidoglycan. This mesh-like structure gives the bacterium its shape and prevents it from bursting when water enters. Gram-positive bacteria have a thick peptidoglycan wall and stain purple; Gram-negative bacteria have a thin peptidoglycan wall and an outer membrane and stain pink. The outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria blocks many antibiotics, making these infections harder to treat.

Surface structures help bacteria survive and cause disease. Flagella are rotating tails that let bacteria swim toward food (chemotaxis). Pili are hair-like structures that let bacteria stick to surfaces—essential for causing infections and forming biofilms. Capsules are slimy coats that hide bacteria from the immune system; many vaccines target the capsule.

Inside, bacteria have a single circular chromosome in the nucleoid, 70S ribosomes for making proteins, and often plasmids—small DNA circles that carry extra genes like antibiotic resistance. Some bacteria form endospores—tough, dormant structures that can survive boiling, radiation, and disinfectants for centuries.

Antibiotics target bacterial structures that are different from human structures. Penicillin blocks the enzymes that cross-link peptidoglycan, weakening the cell wall. Tetracycline and erythromycin block bacterial ribosomes. Quinolones block DNA replication. Gram-negative bacteria are often resistant to many antibiotics because their outer membrane blocks drug entry.

Understanding bacterial structure helps us understand how they live, how they cause disease, and how to treat infections. It also explains why some bacteria are hard to kill and why we need to use antibiotics carefully to prevent resistance.
